item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Noting that a recent edit was reverted due to not actually correcting an error.

PTF stands for Provisional Task Force. Not all task forces are MTFs. Please don't make an edit like this without consulting staff first.

item.title: Random thing I noticed
item.content:encoded:
It could just be some lump of twisted time thrown together by some type blue who got out of their depth.

I sense something wrong the sentence and want to fix it but I don't know what is meant to be in place of blue1.

Aside from that, +1 from me brother.

Footnotes
1. I'm assuming that's what's wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had to skim. -1

I went back to it and didn't find enough to reverse that judgement. One nitpick: nowhere in the article do you describe anything about a -B until it pops up in the last section.

Concept is… a bit like SCP-451 if he weren't dumb as a brick. But the focus is on the technical side, and it fails at that b/c the researchers never do figure out the technical side. Feels like there's a full course meal served up, but instead of letting me eat, it got snatched back from under my teeth.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I liked how this one jumped up in ratings after some dude on /r/dmfs19 made a meme about it.

This is why I plug my SCPs with memes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Intriguing (if a bit hackneyed) concept, good performance.

They say any bad idea can be saved by outstanding execution. This is an okay idea saved by okay execution. Still, it kept me hooked and managed to creep me out by the end, so take my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Bit late to the party, I suppose, but I like this.

At first, the concept of a sentient SCP being very dangerous being also fundamentally cooperative and reasonable until the scientists make a wrong move seems like yet another "oh, let's be nice to the skip or it'll tear us apart" mary-sue. But in this case, the danger is completely unrelated to whether the scientists have upset the SCP—it's coming from the time anomaly itself.

I'd be interested in some experiment logs implying that the phenomenon itself might not be fully stable—whether it might, if poked and prodded too much, destroy whoever/whatever the researchers have been communicating with inside that time anomaly. If inserted between the relatively safe early experiments and the oh-shit moment when two staff disappear, that sort of experiment might serve as foreshadowing and make the transition a bit more gradual.

item.title: Update
item.content:encoded: Thanks for the feedback so far. Made some considerable adjustments to the article, with a rework to the skip’s properties as well as an additional experiment to ease into the final observation.

item.title: Re: So close...
item.content:encoded: Very much agree with this. The concept of this one is really neat, but the third experiment log suddenly going "oh shit this thing can destroy time" when the previous two made it seem mostly harmless was really jarring. There's no sense of progression or steady increase in level of danger, it just goes straight from 0 to end-of-the-world in the span of a single log, and it really makes the reveal feel kinda meaningless. Also I personally think the redaction in the third log is unnecessary and doesn't really add anything to the article — if anything it kinda detracts from it by adding to the jarring-ness of the sudden transition. If you change anything about this I think you should at least remove that redaction, but I really think the article would benefit from a few more test logs that smooth the transition from harmless to world-ending while creating suspense to make the whole thing have more weight to it.

item.title: So close...
item.content:encoded: I really want to upvote this; its a great idea, and for the most part its really well written. I just don't like the leap from 'Cordial and friendly' to suddenly 'dangerous if we push it- end of time scenario'. Its too sudden a leap. If you can weave that idea into article a little more that'd be a lot better. Just provide some better explanation for that threat level, and It'll definitely get my upvote. So no vote for now, but keep at it!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On passive voice:

Scientists always write reports in passive voice. "The substrate was then washed with ethanol and filtered under suction." Touches of passive voice can help cement the scientific tone of an article. Too much passive voice weakens prose. Some passive voice makes an SCP believable.

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded:
(account banned)/OnkleJimble, the comments telling an author to disregard commentors' responses are unwarranted. Just because you disagree with someone else's review doesn't mean that they're automatically wrong and shouldn't be listened to, especially when an article is in the negative ratings.

Even if someone dislikes something due to personal pet peeves, that's still something that the author should take into account while writing, rather than just disregarding entirely. There are only so many times an author can just shrug and say "sure, I'll take the downvote, that's your pet peeve" before an article slips near deletion range.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Imo, disregard what the first commenter said about this being an unoriginal idea. This is remarkably original. I’d say it’s at risk of being too much so, rather than not enough. Also, disregard what another commenter said about“seemingly” That is a perfectly valid way to document, especially in this case. Plus, everyone has a different pet peeve, and you’ll always hit one.

The criticisms are spot on though too; it’s really the grammar and the lack of some development. For example, how the Foundation was able to capture this. It is a painfully glaring handwave, and absolutely needs to be explained.

Please refine this. Submit again if you have to. Fixing the issues will get you in the positives and I would be very glad to see this have a home on the mainlist.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No vote for now, but if you fixed a few minor things it would become a +1. First off, the writing can be a bit choppy at times, but that’s probably just me being picky. The concept is interesting, but I think you should expand upon it a bit more. The “It’s not what it seems” vibe is unsettling, but a further test log, incident log, or even request for reclassification could add to it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
As others have pointed out, the experiment logs are fantastic! I think the note at the bottom would be more impactful if there was an additional experiment log demonstrating the reality-warping danger of interaction with the object. Unfortunately, the description doesn't really do it for me. The wording needs to be more deterministic than the use of "seemingly" implies. The description could also be clearer about the mechanics of the object's interaction with the world.

Overall, +1 from me. The article has great potential, but the description and build up to the final note need work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept, but I'm novoting. The article needs copy-editing. There are lots of apparently unintentional spacing and punctuation errors.

Also, I'd recommend rewriting to eliminate as much passive voice as possible. SCPs tend to overflow with sentences like "[Noun] was to be [verb]ed…" or "[Noun] should be [verb]ed…" It gets a bit old.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i like the concept, but the writing has a lot of errors and the concept is not thoroughly explained enough, i think. not quite sure how to fix that

you should probably include something about how SCP-4055 was recovered in the first place, and in terms of writing issues just go through and check spelling, case, and grammar. remove all opinionated words (except for during interviews or in the notes at the end of tests, stuff like that) you did a pretty good job with clinical tone but not quite there yet

additionally you should probably expand on this "ZK-class end of time scenario" and why exactly SCP-4055 poses this threat, as Blackjack001 said

anyways, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The last log was very good. Also the hidden researcher's notes, I LOVED. I would like this SCP to stay on the site. I was able to make out what it was but a more concise description and some better writing would definitely improve it. Also, it's generally referred to as a ZK-class end-of-reality scenario. Also, as much as I like the last log, we just need more reasoning behind why he thinks this would happen other than the fact that the SCP can alter time.

+1 for now, but if you want more up-votes, you'll need to trim it down a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm gonna be perfectly honest with you: the description did not help me understand what this object was.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it…It was pretty "Meh" at best compared to other SCPs but the concept is absolutely great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
There's a couple reasons I downvoted this.

SCP-4055 is a seemingly humanoid entity existing in a three second “radius” around consensus time.

The word "seems" or "seemingly" is a giant pet peeve of mine, and terrible styling in my eyes.

More importantly, I feel like the entire anomaly is a set up for the end-note, and there's not a lot of set up that gets us there.

It's not…incompentently written, but it's not really doing much beyond setting up a single observation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
God, this SCP seemed somewhat unoriginal at first but reading that note at the end and the final experiment log legit scared me. Very interesting, I enjoyed reading it…but you forgot the vote box.

I'll give this a +1…when I can.

Edit: Added the vote box while I was typing! Good job!
